questions.prompt: Production is broken. Recent deploy caused it. Default response?

choices.text: Rollback first, fix later. Restore service to working state immediately — the previous deploy is already built and known-good; promoting it back (Vercel: one-click; tag-based: trigger rollback workflow with prior version) takes seconds and returns users to a working app. Then diagnose calmly without time pressure. Trying to debug and forward-fix a live incident under stress produces hasty patches and second-incidents. Rollback is faster AND safer for the vast majority of incidents. Practice the rollback workflow monthly so the 3 AM execution is muscle memory, not first-time use.

questions.explanation: Rollback first restores service immediately. Diagnose calmly afterward.

questions.prompt: Bad deploy included a schema migration that dropped a column. Code rollback?

choices.text: Code rollback alone won't work — old code expects the dropped column to exist, will fail on queries referencing it. Either: (1) hotfix forward to address the actual bug (fast, but the schema is now changed); (2) re-add the column via migration (might lose data), then roll back code. The deeper lesson: migrations should be BACKWARD-COMPATIBLE to enable rollback. Split breaking schema changes: Deploy 1 adds new column (old code still works); Deploy 2 uses new column (old code stops working); Deploy 3 drops old column. Between deploys, both code versions work — rollback within any window is safe. Migrations that drop or rename in a single deploy create a rollback trap.

questions.explanation: Code rollback breaks on missing column. Either hotfix forward or re-add column first. Lesson: backward-compatible migrations.
